does: This paper develops a multi-agent LLM defense framework that detects and neutralizes prompt injection attacks in real-time through coordinated agent pipelines. The authors implement two architectural approaches—a sequential chain-of-agents design and a hierarchical coordinator-based system—to intercept malicious instructions embedded in user inputs before they can override system prompts. They evaluate their framework against 55 unique prompt injection attacks organized into 8 categories, testing 400 total attack instances across ChatGLM and Llama2 platforms. The results show the multi-agent pipeline reduces Attack Success Rate from 30% and 20% baseline (ChatGLM and Llama2 respectively) down to 0% across all tested scenarios, demonstrating complete mitigation of the attack categories evaluated.
connects: This work addresses a critical vulnerability in LLM deployment that has emerged as systems like GPT-4 and open-source models become widely integrated into production applications. It builds on prior research in adversarial attacks against language models and extends recent work on prompt injection vulnerabilities by proposing an active defense rather than reactive post-hoc corrections. The multi-agent approach draws inspiration from ensemble and cooperative AI methods that leverage multiple specialized models to solve complex problems, adapting these techniques to the security domain. The paper connects to broader literature on input validation, instruction-following robustness, and defensive mechanisms for neural systems, while addressing limitations of simpler single-model defenses that lack the coordination and specialization needed to handle diverse attack patterns.
enables: This framework enables more secure deployment of LLMs in high-stakes applications where prompt injection poses unacceptable risks, such as customer service systems, automated decision-making, and information retrieval pipelines. The multi-agent architecture provides a foundation for building adaptive defenses that can specialize in detecting different attack categories, potentially leading to more efficient and targeted security mechanisms. Future work could explore how these defense pipelines scale to emerging attack variants and novel injection techniques not present in the evaluation set, investigate whether adversarial training can further harden the agents, and examine trade-offs between security and latency in real-time deployment. The approach also opens directions for studying the theoretical properties of multi-agent defense systems and developing formal verification methods for prompt security.
